		"requires_user_action": false,
		"arguments": {
			"skill": "task_management",
			"format": "full"
		},
		"result": {
			"id": "task_management",
			"name": "Task Management",
			"type": "skill",
			"notes": [
				"Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.",
				"Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context."
			],
			"format": "full",
			"summary": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
			"version": "tool-registry/ddbb4c1e",
			"examples": [
				{
					"next_steps": [
						"Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.",
						"Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.",
						"Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.",
						"If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.",
						"Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.",
						"Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`"
					],
					"description": "Track a real follow-up the user must do later"
				},
				{
					"next_steps": [
						"If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.",
						"Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`"
					],
					"description": "Create the next drafting task from a progress update"
				},
				{
					"next_steps": [
						"If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.",
						"Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.",
						"When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.",
						"If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.",
						"If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.",
						"Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.",
						"Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`",
						"Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`"
					],
					"description": "Update an existing task status and owner safely"
				},
				{
					"next_steps": [
						"Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.",
						"Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.",
						"If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.",
						"If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question."
					],
					"description": "Guard against empty task writes"
				}
			],
			"markdown": "# Task Management\n\nTask workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## When to Use\n- Decide whether a user request should become a task at all\n- Create a task for future human work\n- Assign or reassign task ownership\n- Update task state, dates, or priority\n- Place a task under the right plan, goal, or milestone\n\n## Workflow\n1. Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.\n2. If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.\n3. For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.\n4. Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.\n5. Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.\n6. For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.\n7. Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.\n8. After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters.\n\n## Related Tools\n- `onto.task.create`\n- `onto.task.get`\n- `onto.task.list`\n- `onto.task.search`\n- `onto.task.update`\n- `onto.plan.get`\n- `onto.goal.get`\n- `onto.milestone.get`\n\n## Guardrails\n- Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.\n- Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.\n- Do not use invalid task states such as open.\n- Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.\n- If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy.\n\n## Examples\n### Track a real follow-up the user must do later\n- Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.\n- Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.\n- Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.\n- If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.\n- Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.\n- Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`\n### Create the next drafting task from a progress update\n- If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.\n- Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`\n### Update an existing task status and owner safely\n- If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.\n- Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.\n- When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.\n- If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.\n- If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.\n- Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.\n- Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`\n- Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`\n### Guard against empty task writes\n- Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.\n- Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.\n- If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.\n- If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question.\n\n## Notes\n- Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.\n- Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context.",
			"workflow": [
				"1) Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.",
				"2) If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.",
				"3) For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.",
				"4) Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.",
				"5) Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.",
				"6) For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.",
				"7) Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.",
				"8) After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters."
			],
			"guardrails": [
				"Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.",
				"Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.",
				"Do not use invalid task states such as open.",
				"Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.",
				"If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy."
			],
			"description": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",

			],

							"Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.",
							"Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Track a real follow-up the user must do later"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.",
							"Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Create the next drafting task from a progress update"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.",
							"Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.",
							"When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.",
							"If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.",
							"If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.",
							"Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.",
							"Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`",
							"Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Update an existing task status and owner safely"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.",
							"Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.",
							"If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.",
							"If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question."
						],
						"description": "Guard against empty task writes"
					}
				],
				"markdown": "# Task Management\n\nTask workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## When to Use\n- Decide whether a user request should become a task at all\n- Create a task for future human work\n- Assign or reassign task ownership\n- Update task state, dates, or priority\n- Place a task under the right plan, goal, or milestone\n\n## Workflow\n1. Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.\n2. If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.\n3. For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.\n4. Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.\n5. Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.\n6. For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.\n7. Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.\n8. After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters.\n\n## Related Tools\n- `onto.task.create`\n- `onto.task.get`\n- `onto.task.list`\n- `onto.task.search`\n- `onto.task.update`\n- `onto.plan.get`\n- `onto.goal.get`\n- `onto.milestone.get`\n\n## Guardrails\n- Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.\n- Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.\n- Do not use invalid task states such as open.\n- Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.\n- If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy.\n\n## Examples\n### Track a real follow-up the user must do later\n- Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.\n- Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.\n- Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.\n- If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.\n- Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.\n- Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`\n### Create the next drafting task from a progress update\n- If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.\n- Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`\n### Update an existing task status and owner safely\n- If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.\n- Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.\n- When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.\n- If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.\n- If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.\n- Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.\n- Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`\n- Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`\n### Guard against empty task writes\n- Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.\n- Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.\n- If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.\n- If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question.\n\n## Notes\n- Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.\n- Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context.",
				"workflow": [
					"1) Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.",
					"2) If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.",
					"3) For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.",
					"4) Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.",
					"5) Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.",
					"6) For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.",
					"7) Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.",
					"8) After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters."
				],
				"guardrails": [
					"Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.",
					"Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.",
					"Do not use invalid task states such as open.",
					"Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.",
					"If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy."
				],
				"description": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"related_ops": [
					"onto.task.create",
					"onto.task.get",
					"onto.task.list",
					"onto.task.search",
					"onto.task.update",
					"onto.plan.get",

				],
				"when_to_use": [

					"Create a task for future human work",

				],
				"legacy_paths": ["onto.task.skill", "task.skill", "tasks.skill"]
			},
			"created_at": "2026-04-16T15:25:06.362772+00:00"
		},

	},
	"result": {
		"id": "task_management",
		"name": "Task Management",
		"type": "skill",
		"notes": [
			"Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.",
			"Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context."
		],
		"format": "full",
		"summary": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
		"version": "tool-registry/ddbb4c1e",
		"examples": [
			{
				"next_steps": [
					"Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.",
					"Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.",
					"Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.",
					"If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.",
					"Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.",
					"Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`"
				],
				"description": "Track a real follow-up the user must do later"
			},
			{
				"next_steps": [
					"If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.",
					"Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`"
				],
				"description": "Create the next drafting task from a progress update"
			},
			{
				"next_steps": [
					"If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.",
					"Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.",
					"When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.",
					"If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.",
					"If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.",
					"Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.",
					"Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`",
					"Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`"
				],
				"description": "Update an existing task status and owner safely"
			},
			{
				"next_steps": [
					"Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.",
					"Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.",
					"If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.",
					"If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question."
				],
				"description": "Guard against empty task writes"
			}
		],
		"markdown": "# Task Management\n\nTask workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## When to Use\n- Decide whether a user request should become a task at all\n- Create a task for future human work\n- Assign or reassign task ownership\n- Update task state, dates, or priority\n- Place a task under the right plan, goal, or milestone\n\n## Workflow\n1. Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.\n2. If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.\n3. For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.\n4. Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.\n5. Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.\n6. For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.\n7. Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.\n8. After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters.\n\n## Related Tools\n- `onto.task.create`\n- `onto.task.get`\n- `onto.task.list`\n- `onto.task.search`\n- `onto.task.update`\n- `onto.plan.get`\n- `onto.goal.get`\n- `onto.milestone.get`\n\n## Guardrails\n- Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.\n- Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.\n- Do not use invalid task states such as open.\n- Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.\n- If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy.\n\n## Examples\n### Track a real follow-up the user must do later\n- Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.\n- Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.\n- Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.\n- If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.\n- Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.\n- Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`\n### Create the next drafting task from a progress update\n- If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.\n- Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`\n### Update an existing task status and owner safely\n- If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.\n- Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.\n- When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.\n- If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.\n- If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.\n- Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.\n- Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`\n- Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`\n### Guard against empty task writes\n- Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.\n- Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.\n- If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.\n- If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question.\n\n## Notes\n- Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.\n- Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context.",
		"workflow": [
			"1) Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.",
			"2) If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.",
			"3) For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.",
			"4) Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.",
			"5) Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.",
			"6) For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.",
			"7) Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.",
			"8) After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters."
		],
		"guardrails": [
			"Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.",
			"Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.",
			"Do not use invalid task states such as open.",
			"Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.",
			"If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy."
		],
		"description": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",

		"project_id": "54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c",
		"context_type": "project",
		"project_name": "The Last Ember",
		"lite_sections": [
			{
				"id": "identity_mission",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 602,
				"title": "Identity and Mission",
				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
				"estimatedTokens": 151
			},
			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 1019,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.",
				"estimatedTokens": 255
			},
			{
				"id": "safety_data_rules",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 566,
				"title": "Safety and Data Rules",
				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",
				"estimatedTokens": 142
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 3767,
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.static_capability_skill_catalog",
				"content": "Think in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"estimatedTokens": 942
			},
			{
				"id": "focus_purpose",
				"kind": "dynamic",
				"chars": 461,
				"slots": {

	"rendered_dump_text": "========================================\nFASTCHAT V2 PROMPT SNAPSHOT\nStream run: 864c9c4d-125c-46f0-9eac-add63719b93b\nSession:    ac79da05-7120-4b63-b3db-6e53b40bc3cc\nContext:    project\nEntity ID:  54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c\nProject ID: 54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\nTools (9): skill_load, tool_search, tool_schema, change_chat_context, get_project_overview, get_onto_project_details, search_project, list_onto_tasks, list_onto_documents\nMessage count: 8\nSystem prompt length: 13363 chars (~3341 tokens)\n========================================\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nSYSTEM PROMPT\n────────────────────────────────────────\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and MESSAGES\n────────────────────────────────────────\n[1] role=system\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete first draft of 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- inside The 		"title": "Magic System Research Notes",
		"content": "# Magic System Research Notes\n\n## Emotion-Based Forging (Inspired by Japanese Sword-Making)\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",
		"type_key": "document.knowledge.research",
		"state_key": "draft",
		"project_id": "54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c",
		"description": "Research notes on the magic system for 'The Last Ember', drawing from Japanese sword-making traditions, emotional influences on weapons, Damascus steel, Celtic mythology, medieval weapons, and world-building elements like Forge Temples and quenching rituals."
	},
	"result": null,
	"tool_result_source": null,
	"linked_tool_execution": null,
	"linked_tool_message": null,

Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",

		"title": "Magic System Research Notes",
		"content": "# Magic System Research Notes\n\n## Emotion-Based Forging (Inspired by Japanese Sword-Making)\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",

Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",
					"children": {
						"children": []
					},
					"type_key": "document.default",
					"state_key": "draft",
					"created_at": "2026-04-16T15:27:29.680543+00:00",
					"created_by": "85eba71b-56c9-4561-9dae-3828cf9922c4",
					"deleted_at": null,
					"project_id": "54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c",
					"updated_at": "2026-04-16T15:27:29.680543+00:00",
					"description": "Research notes on the magic system for 'The Last Ember', drawing from Japanese sword-making traditions, emotional influences on weapons, Damascus steel, Celtic mythology, medieval weapons, and world-building elements like Forge Temples and quenching rituals.",
					"search_vector": "'across':153C,285 'adapt':76C,192 'addit':123C,251 'aethermoor':154C,286 'affect':83C,199 'ancient':127C,256 'anger':88C,206 'area':101C,224 'base':49C,165 'beyond':118C,244 'blade':75C,191 'bodi':155 'build':35B,122C,250 'burn':133C,262 'celtic':28B,108C,233 'complet':147C,278 'damag':90C,208 'damascus':26B,102C,226 'defens':97C,218 'descript':107C,231 'differ':149C,281 'dragon':130C,259 'draw':15B 'element':36B 'elena':78C,194 'ember':14B 'emot':22B,48C,80C,164,196 'emotion-bas':47C,163 'enter':73C,189 'fear':96C,217 'fire':89C,131C,207,260 'forg':38B,50C,82C,125C,151C,166,198,254,283 'found':57C 'god':112C,237 'goibniu':113C,238 'guild':136C,266 'heal':94C,214 'hierarchi':137C,267 'ice/frost':92C,211 'idea':67C,183 'influenc':23B 'inspir':51C,167 'interest':58C,174 'japanes':17B,53C,61C,169,177 'joy':93C,213 'last':13B 'like':37B 'magic':1A,9B,43C,144C,157,275 'make':20B,56C,64C,172,180 'markdown':156 'mediev':30B,116C,242 'mytholog':29B,109C,234 'n':161,162,204,205,209,212,216,220,221,225,232,239,246,247,252,263,270,279 'nfound':173 'note':4A,6B,46C,160 'ongo':99C,222 'parallel':59C,175 'pattern':104C,228 'properti':87C,95C,203,215 'quench':41B,141C,272 'region':148C,280 'research':3A,5B,45C,100C,159,223 'ritual':42B,142C,273 'shield':98C,219 'site':128C,257 'smith':70C,111C,134C,186,236,264 'sorrow':91C,210 'spirit':72C,188 'steel':27B,103C,227 'still':132C,261 'sword':19B,55C,63C,119C,171,179,245 'sword-mak':18B,54C,62C,170,178 'system':2A,10B,44C,158 'techniqu':152C,284 'templ':39B,126C,255 'tradit':21B,65C,139C,181,269 'type':114C,240 'visual':106C,230 'weapon':25B,31B,85C,117C,145C,201,243,276 'world':34B,121C,249 'world-build':33B,120C,248"

				"description": "Initial research captured:\n- Emotion-based properties: Anger (fire), Sorrow (ice), Joy (healing), Fear (shields).\n- Inspirations: Japanese sword-making, Damascus steel, Celtic smith gods (Goibniu), medieval weapons.\n- World-building: Forge Temples, Smith's Guild, Quenching Ritual, regional techniques.\n\nSee linked document: Magic System Research Notes for full details.\n\nNext: Refine into core rules and integrate into chapter outlines.\"\n<parameter name=\"update_strategy\">replace",

		"title": "Magic System Research Notes",
		"content": "# Magic System Research Notes\n\n## Emotion-Based Forging (Inspired by Japanese Sword-Making)\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",
		"type_key": "document.knowledge.research",
		"state_key": "draft",
		"project_id": "54ff9023-7c23-440a-ada3-469fb412661c",
		"description": "Research notes on the magic system for 'The Last Ember', drawing from Japanese sword-making traditions, emotional influences on weapons, Damascus steel, Celtic mythology, medieval weapons, and world-building elements like Forge Temples and quenching rituals."
	},
	"result": {
		"message": "Created ontology document \"Magic System Research Notes\"",
		"document": {

				"body_markdown": "# Magic System Research Notes\n\n## Emotion-Based Forging (Inspired by Japanese Sword-Making)\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- **Anger** = fire damage\n- **Sorrow** = ice/frost\n- **Joy** = healing properties\n- **Fear** = defensive shields\n\n## Ongoing Research Areas\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n## World-Building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor"
			},

#### Request Message

```text
I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.

Main plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.

Need to:

- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)
- Create magic system based on metal and fire
- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor
- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)
- Outline first three chapters
- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques
- Design the prophecy that drives the plot
```

Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent focus:\n- The user is trying to create a new BuildOS project right now.\n- No existing project or focus entity exists yet; treat the user message as the source of truth for the initial project.\n\nProject creation means:\n- Turn a rough idea into the smallest valid project structure with a clear name, type_key, description/props, and only the entities and relationships the user actually described.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when a required detail blocks a safe create payload.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nProject creation scope:\n- This chat is in project_create mode before a project exists.\n- No existing project graph or focus entity is preloaded by default.\n- Use the user message as the source of truth for the new project; fetch schema guidance only if a create field is uncertain.\n\n## Project Creation Boundaries\n\nCreation boundaries:\n- Use the user idea and project_create mode state as the working context.\n- Do not load existing workspace/project data just to begin project creation.\n- Prefer the project_creation skill for multi-step creation, then call the direct create tool once the payload is ready.\n\nLoaded:\n- project_create mode before a project exists\n\nNot preloaded:\n- existing project graph unless explicitly provided\n\nFetch only when needed:\n- schema details for uncertain create payload fields\n\n## Current Tool Surface\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project"
		},
		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
		}
	],
	"tool_definitions": [
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "skill_load",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["skill"],
					"properties": {
						"skill": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Canonical skill id such as \"project_creation\", \"calendar_management\", \"task_management\", or \"document_workspace\". Legacy dotted skill aliases also work during migration."
						},
						"format": {
							"enum": ["short", "full"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short returns a compact summary; full returns the full playbook."
						},
						"include_examples": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include examples when available."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Load one BuildOS skill playbook by skill id. Use this when the task is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong and you need workflow guidance before choosing tools."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "tool_search",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"kind": {
							"enum": ["read", "write"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional read/write filter. Prefer this when you know whether you need a mutation or a lookup."
						},
						"group": {
							"enum": ["onto", "util", "cal"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional top-level tool family filter. Use this to narrow the search space once you know the tool family."
						},
						"limit": {
							"type": "integer",
							"description": "Maximum number of matches to return."
						},
						"query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Natural-language description of the operation you need, not project/task content. Good examples: \"workspace overview\", \"update existing task state\", or \"move document in tree\". Bad example: \"chapter 3 plans\" when you are trying to find project data."
						},
						"entity": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional entity filter such as \"task\", \"project\", or \"document\". For example: entity=\"task\" with kind=\"write\" for task mutations."
						},
						"capability": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional BuildOS capability id or path such as \"overview\", \"project_creation\", or \"capabilities.calendar\". Prefer this when the capability family is already clear."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Discover candidate BuildOS tools on demand. Use this only when the exact op is still unknown after context and skill guidance. Search for the operation you need, not workspace data."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "tool_schema",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["op"],
					"properties": {
						"op": {
							"type": "string",
							"minLength": 1,
							"description": "Canonical operation name such as \"onto.task.update\", \"util.project.overview\", or \"cal.event.create\"."
						},
						"include_schema": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include the full JSON schema for the op arguments."
						},
						"include_examples": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include example calls when available."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Return the exact schema, required arguments, examples, and usage guidance for one canonical BuildOS op."
			}
		},
		{
			"name": "create_onto_project",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "create_onto_project",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project", "entities", "relationships"],
					"properties": {
						"meta": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"model": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"confidence": {
									"type": "number",
									"maximum": 1,
									"minimum": 0
								},
								"suggested_facets": {
									"type": "object",
									"properties": {
										"scale": {
											"type": "string"
										},
										"stage": {
											"type": "string"
										},
										"context": {
											"type": "string"
										}
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Generation metadata"
						},
						"project": {
							"type": "object",
							"required": ["name", "type_key"],
							"properties": {
								"name": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Project name"
								},
								"props": {
									"type": "object",
									"properties": {
										"facets": {
											"type": "object",
											"properties": {
												"scale": {
													"enum": [
														"micro",
														"small",
														"medium",
														"large",
														"epic"
													],
													"type": "string"
												},
												"stage": {
													"enum": [
														"discovery",
														"planning",
														"execution",
														"launch",
														"maintenance",
														"complete"
													],
													"type": "string"
												},
												"context": {
													"enum": [
														"personal",
														"client",
														"commercial",
														"internal",
														"open_source",
														"community",
														"academic",
														"nonprofit",
														"startup"
													],
													"type": "string"
												}
											},
											"description": "Optional standard facets"
										}
									},
									"description": "Optional JSONB properties from the user message."
								},
								"end_at": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "ISO end date"
								},
								"start_at": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "ISO start date"
								},
								"type_key": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Starts with project.; e.g. project.creative.novel."
								},
								"state_key": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Initial state. Valid: planning, active, completed, cancelled."
								},
								"description": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Project description"
								}
							},
							"description": "Project definition"
						},
						"entities": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"required": ["temp_id", "kind"],
								"properties": {
									"uri": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"kind": {
										"enum": [
											"goal",
											"milestone",
											"plan",
											"task",
											"document",
											"risk",
											"metric",
											"source"
										],
										"type": "string"
									},
									"name": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"text": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"unit": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"props": {
										"type": "object"
									},
									"title": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"due_at": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"impact": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"content": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"temp_id": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"end_date": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"priority": {
										"type": "number"
									},
									"start_at": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"type_key": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"state_key": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"definition": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"start_date": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"description": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"probability": {
										"type": "number"
									},
									"target_date": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"snapshot_uri": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"target_value": {
										"type": "number"
									},
									"body_markdown": {
										"type": "string"
									},
									"measurement_criteria": {
										"type": "string"
									}
								}
							},
							"default": [],
							"description": "Entity list. Labels: goal/plan/metric name; task/milestone/document/risk title; requirement text; source uri."
						},
						"relationships": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"oneOf": [
									{
										"type": "array",
										"items": {
											"type": "object",
											"required": ["temp_id", "kind"],
											"properties": {
												"kind": {
													"type": "string"
												},
												"temp_id": {
													"type": "string"
												}
											}
										},
										"maxItems": 2,
										"minItems": 2
									},
									{
										"type": "object",
										"required": ["from", "to"],
										"properties": {
											"to": {
												"type": "object",
												"required": ["temp_id", "kind"],
												"properties": {
													"kind": {
														"type": "string"
													},
													"temp_id": {
														"type": "string"
													}
												}
											},
											"rel": {
												"type": "string"
											},
											"from": {
												"type": "object",
												"required": ["temp_id", "kind"],
												"properties": {
													"kind": {
														"type": "string"
													},
													"temp_id": {
														"type": "string"
													}
												}
											},
											"intent": {
												"enum": ["containment", "semantic"],
												"type": "string"
											}
										}
									}
								]
							},
							"default": [],
							"description": "Required directional temp_id connections. Items can be [from, to] or { from, to, rel?, intent? }."
						},
						"clarifications": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",
								"required": ["key", "question", "required"],
								"properties": {
									"key": {
										"type": "string",
										"description": "Unique key for this question"
									},
									"choices": {
										"type": "array",
										"items": {
											"type": "string"
										},
										"description": "Optional predefined choices"
									},
									"question": {
										"type": "string",
										"description": "The question to ask"
									},
									"required": {
										"type": "boolean",
										"description": "Whether answer is required"
									},
									"help_text": {
										"type": "string"
									}
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional critical missing-info questions"
						},
						"context_document": {
							"type": "object",
							"required": ["title", "content"],
							"properties": {
								"props": {
									"type": "object",
									"description": "Additional metadata"
								},
								"title": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"content": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Markdown body"
								},
								"type_key": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Defaults to document.context.project"
								},
								"state_key": {
									"type": "string",
									"description": "Document state"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional document.context.project linked to the project."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Create a project from a ProjectSpec. Always include project, entities, relationships; use [] when empty.\nHard rules: project.type_key starts with project. e.g. project.creative.novel. Entity labels: goal/plan/metric name; task/milestone/document/risk title; requirement text; source uri. Milestone needs due_at.\nInfer name/type_key when clear; ask one clarification only if too vague. Start minimal: one goal for an explicit outcome, tasks for explicit actions, plans/milestones only for phases, dates, or workstreams.\nExtract concrete details into description/props. Use temp_id + kind refs for relationships."
			},

			},
			"description": "Create a project from a ProjectSpec. Always include project, entities, relationships; use [] when empty.\nHard rules: project.type_key starts with project. e.g. project.creative.novel. Entity labels: goal/plan/metric name; task/milestone/document/risk title; requirement text; source uri. Milestone needs due_at.\nInfer name/type_key when clear; ask one clarification only if too vague. Start minimal: one goal for an explicit outcome, tasks for explicit actions, plans/milestones only for phases, dates, or workstreams.\nExtract concrete details into description/props. Use temp_id + kind refs for relationships."
		}
	],
